questions.question: A homeowner uses their basement as a home office for a new business instead of renting it to a student for \$400 per month. What is the monthly opportunity cost of this decision?
options: The cost of setting up the home office
The profit earned from the new business
The combined value of rent and business profit
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative forgone. The homeowner sacrifices \$400 per month in rental income by using the space for business.

questions.question: A logistics firm purchases a fleet of electric delivery vans. Which factor of production does this fleet represent?
questions.explanation: Capital consists of man-made tools, equipment, and structures used to produce goods and services. The delivery vans are capital assets.

questions.question: A government agency determines that the country should produce more renewable energy and fewer fossil fuels. Which basic economic question is being decided?
questions.explanation: Deciding the mix of goods and services to allocate resources toward addresses the fundamental 'What to produce?' question.

questions.question: An analysis examines how a change in the price of lumber affects the number of new houses built by a specific construction company. Is this Microeconomics or Macroeconomics?
questions.explanation: This focuses on a single firm and a specific market, which falls within the scope of microeconomic analysis.

questions.question: Identify the type of economic statement: 'A tax on sugary drinks will reduce the total consumption of soda by 12%.'
questions.explanation: This is an objective, testable prediction that can be verified with data. It describes 'what is' rather than making a value judgment.

questions.question: A country discovers a way to produce both agricultural and industrial goods using 20% less energy. How is this shown on the PPF?
options: A movement along the existing PPF curve
An outward shift of the entire PPF curve
A pivot along the agricultural axis only
No change to the PPF curve
questions.explanation: General technological improvements that benefit all sectors increase maximum possible output for all goods, shifting the entire PPF outward.

questions.question: A curved PPF shows that moving from clothing to electronics requires giving up increasingly larger amounts of clothing. What economic law does this illustrate?
options: The Law of Increasing Opportunity Cost
The Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility
questions.explanation: A concave PPF reflects that resources are specialized. As production shifts, the opportunity cost rises because less suitable resources must be used.

questions.question: In one day, Country A produces 50 units of software. Country B produces 30 units of software. Which country has the absolute advantage in software?
questions.explanation: Absolute advantage means producing more output with the same resources. 50 units exceeds 30 units per day.

questions.question: Country A's cost for 1 car is 5 tons of grain. Country B's cost for 1 car is 3 tons of grain. Which country has the comparative advantage in car production?
questions.explanation: Country B sacrifices only 3 tons of grain per car vs. Country A's 5 tons. Country B has the lower opportunity cost for cars.

questions.question: The opportunity cost of a laptop is 2 smartphones in Japan and 5 smartphones in South Korea. What is the minimum price Japan would accept to export one laptop?
questions.explanation: Japan will only trade if the export price exceeds its domestic production cost. Japan's cost is 2 smartphones, so it needs a price above 2 to gain from trade.

questions.question: The price of a popular brand of sneakers falls, and more people are seen wearing them. Which law explains this relationship?
questions.explanation: The Law of Demand describes the inverse relationship between price and quantity demanded. Lower prices lead to higher quantities purchased.

questions.question: How does a successful advertising campaign for a product affect its demand curve?
options: It causes a movement along the demand curve
It shifts the demand curve to the right
It shifts the demand curve to the left
It has no effect on the demand curve
questions.explanation: Advertising changes consumer tastes and preferences, increasing the quantity demanded at every price level and shifting the demand curve rightward.

questions.question: The government provides a production grant to all manufacturers of solar panels. How does this affect the supply curve?
options: The supply curve shifts to the left
The supply curve shifts to the right
There is a movement along the supply curve
The supply curve is unaffected by grants
questions.explanation: Grants and subsidies lower the effective cost of production, encouraging firms to supply more at every price. The supply curve shifts rightward.

questions.question: The Income Elasticity of Demand for a product is 1.5. How is this product classified?
options: A good with zero income elasticity
questions.explanation: Normal goods with a YED greater than 1 are classified as luxuries. Demand for these goods grows faster than income.

questions.question: A market is in equilibrium. Demand for the product increases while supply stays the same. What happens to equilibrium price and quantity?
questions.explanation: A rightward shift in demand along a stable supply curve moves the equilibrium point up and to the right, increasing both price and quantity.

questions.question: A 15% increase in bus ticket prices results in a 3% decrease in passengers. Calculate the Price Elasticity of Demand.
questions.explanation: PED = % Change in Qd / % Change in P = 3% / 15% = 0.2. Demand is inelastic.

questions.question: Why does a house tend to have a higher Price Elasticity of Demand than a pencil?
options: Houses have more close substitutes than pencils do
Houses take up a much larger share of a consumer's total budget
Pencils are necessities while houses are luxury items
Houses are more durable goods than pencils are
questions.explanation: The proportion of income spent on a good affects price sensitivity. Expensive items like houses represent a large budget share, making consumers more responsive to price changes.

questions.question: Income decreases by 8% and the demand for generic-brand cereal increases by 12%. What is the YED and what type of good is it?
questions.explanation: YED = 12% / –8% = –1.5. A negative YED means demand rises when income falls, which is characteristic of an inferior good.

questions.question: The cross-price elasticity of demand between printers and ink cartridges is –0.8. What is the relationship between these goods?
questions.explanation: A negative cross-price elasticity indicates that the goods are used together. When the price of one rises, demand for the other falls.

questions.question: A firm increases its output by 20% in response to a 5% increase in market price. What is the Price Elasticity of Supply?
questions.explanation: PES = % Change in Qs / % Change in P = 20% / 5% = 4.0. Supply is highly elastic.

questions.question: How does excess capacity (idle machinery and underutilized labor) affect a firm's Price Elasticity of Supply?
options: It has no effect on supply elasticity
questions.explanation: Firms with idle resources can quickly increase production when prices rise, making their supply more responsive and elastic.

questions.question: A restaurant owner can change the number of waiters daily but can only expand the kitchen once a year. In the short run, the kitchen is considered what type of factor?
questions.explanation: Fixed factors of production cannot be changed in the short run. The kitchen size is fixed, while the number of waiters is variable.

questions.question: A startup founder leaves a \$100,000 job to start a company. After one year, accounting profit is \$120,000. What is the Economic Profit?
questions.explanation: Economic Profit = Accounting Profit – Implicit Costs = \$120,000 – \$100,000 = \$20,000.

questions.question: A firm benefits from marketing economies of scale. What happens to its average advertising cost per unit as it produces more?
questions.explanation: Larger firms can spread fixed marketing and advertising costs over a larger volume of sales, reducing the average cost per unit sold.

questions.question: Many buyers and sellers trade a homogeneous product, and firms are price takers. Which market structure is this?
questions.explanation: Perfect competition features many buyers and sellers, identical products, free entry and exit, and firms that cannot influence the market price.

questions.question: A firm's Marginal Cost is \$15 and its Marginal Revenue is \$20. What should the firm do to maximize profit?
questions.explanation: As long as MR exceeds MC, each additional unit produced adds to total profit. The firm should expand output until MR = MC.

questions.question: The price of the final product sold by a firm increases. How does this affect the firm's demand for labor?
options: The demand for labor remains unchanged
questions.explanation: Higher product prices increase the Marginal Revenue Product of labor (MRP = MP × Price), shifting the labor demand curve rightward.

questions.question: Why might a surgeon earn significantly more than a supermarket cashier?
options: Cashiers work longer hours than surgeons on average
Surgeons require extensive training and possess rare skills, which limits labor supply
Supermarkets generate less total revenue than hospitals do
Cashiers have more opportunities for overtime pay than surgeons
questions.explanation: Limited supply of labor relative to high demand for specialized medical skills results in significantly higher equilibrium wages for surgeons.

questions.question: In the five-sector circular flow model, what is the term for money leaving the economy when domestic households buy goods produced abroad?
questions.explanation: Spending on imports represents money flowing out of the domestic circular flow. Imports are a leakage or withdrawal from the economy.

questions.question: GNP is \$900 billion. Income earned by foreigners domestically is \$50 billion. Income earned by citizens abroad is \$70 billion. Calculate GDP.
questions.explanation: GNP = GDP + (Citizen income abroad – Foreigner income domestically). \$900B = GDP + (\$70B – \$50B). GDP = \$900B – \$20B = \$880 billion.

questions.question: Which GDP component includes the value of unsold goods added to a firm's warehouse at the end of the year?
questions.explanation: In national accounting, additions to inventory are treated as investment spending because they represent goods produced but not yet consumed.

questions.question: In the income approach to GDP, what is the term for income earned by self-employed individuals that combines both wages and profit?
questions.explanation: Mixed income accounts for the combined labor and entrepreneurial effort of self-employed individuals who cannot separate their wages from their business profits.

questions.question: Nominal GDP is \$440 billion and the GDP Deflator is 110. Calculate Real GDP.
questions.explanation: Real GDP = (Nominal GDP ÷ GDP Deflator) × 100 = (\$440B ÷ 110) × 100 = \$400 billion.

questions.question: Real GDP grows by 4% while population grows by 6%. What happens to Real GDP per capita?
questions.explanation: Population is growing faster than output, so there is less output available per person. The average standard of living declines.

questions.question: A plant closes because the company moved operations to a country with lower labor costs. Workers cannot find new jobs because their skills are industry-specific. What type of unemployment is this?
questions.explanation: Structural unemployment results from long-term changes in the economy. Workers' specific skills are no longer in demand in the domestic labor market.

questions.question: The price of almost all goods and services in an economy rises by 2% every year. What is this persistent upward trend called?
questions.explanation: Inflation is the sustained increase in the general price level of goods and services over time.

questions.question: What is the term for the unemployment rate at which inflation is stable and the labor market is in equilibrium?
options: The Non-Accelerating Inflation Rate of Unemployment
questions.explanation: NAIRU is the unemployment rate consistent with stable inflation. It is often used interchangeably with the natural rate of unemployment.

questions.question: During unanticipated inflation, who loses more purchasing power: a worker with a cost-of-living adjustment in their contract, or a worker with a fixed nominal wage?
options: The worker with the cost-of-living adjustment
The worker with the fixed nominal wage
Neither loses; inflation affects both the same
questions.explanation: Fixed nominal wages do not rise with inflation, so the real purchasing power of those wages declines. COLA contracts automatically adjust wages upward.

questions.question: A government increases spending on infrastructure projects to stimulate the economy. What type of fiscal policy is this?
questions.explanation: Discretionary fiscal policy requires deliberate legislative action to change government spending levels or tax rates.

questions.question: What is the term for the difference between the number of immigrants and emigrants in a country over a year?
questions.explanation: Net migration is the difference between immigration (people entering) and emigration (people leaving) over a specific period.

questions.question: People can easily carry money in their wallets to make purchases at different locations. Which characteristic of money does this demonstrate?
questions.explanation: Portability means money must be easy to carry and transport to function effectively as a medium of exchange across different locations.

questions.question: An online store lists the price of a laptop as 1.5 Bitcoins. In this case, Bitcoin is functioning as what?
questions.explanation: Bitcoin is being used as the standard measure to express the value of the laptop. Money is serving its unit of account function.

questions.question: What is the primary tool the Central Bank uses as monetary authority to influence the level of inflation?
questions.explanation: Central banks adjust the policy interest rate to influence borrowing costs, aggregate demand, and ultimately the rate of inflation.

questions.question: What is the term for the percentage of total deposits a commercial bank must keep in cash or at the Central Bank?
questions.explanation: The required reserve ratio determines the fraction of deposits banks must hold as reserves, limiting how much money they can create through lending.

questions.question: A person deposits \$2,000 in a savings account with 5% interest compounded annually. How much total interest is earned after exactly two years?
Why is information asymmetry in the health insurance market a problem?
options: Insurers know more about medicine than the patients they insure
It leads to adverse selection, where only high-risk individuals seek coverage
It causes insurance companies to earn excessive profits at the expense of patients
Information asymmetry has no significant effect on insurance markets
questions.explanation: When buyers know more about their health than insurers, prices may rise. Healthy people then drop out, leaving only high-risk individuals in the pool, potentially collapsing the market.

questions.question: The government sets a price ceiling on apartment rents above the current market equilibrium. What is the effect on quantity rented?
options: No effect; the ceiling is not binding
Rents immediately fall to the ceiling level
questions.explanation: A price ceiling is only binding if set below equilibrium. A ceiling above equilibrium does not constrain the market, so no change occurs.

questions.question: What is the common technical definition of a recession?
options: One quarter of negative GDP growth
Two consecutive quarters of declining Real GDP
A full year of economic contraction
Any increase in the unemployment rate
questions.explanation: A recession is commonly defined as two consecutive quarters (six months) of negative economic growth, measured by falling Real GDP.

questions.question: How does political stability contribute to a nation's economic growth?
options: By allowing the government to print more money
By encouraging investment through reduced uncertainty and protected property rights
By eliminating all forms of taxation on businesses
By guaranteeing equal income distribution among all citizens
questions.explanation: Investment requires a stable environment. Political stability reassures both domestic and foreign investors that their assets and expected returns are secure.

questions.question: What is a regressive tax?
options: A tax that increases as income increases
A tax that takes a larger percentage of income from low-income earners
A tax that applies only to luxury goods and services
A tax that is refunded to low-income households at year end
questions.explanation: A regressive tax takes a higher proportion of income from those with lower incomes. Sales taxes are often considered regressive for this reason.
